  "opinion": "I think it's really important to approach conversations on COVID vaccines carefully because they can stir up a lot of emotion, especially these days online. Having this depression thing going on makes me more sensitive to discussions where there's uncertainty or fear involved—it can get overwhelming.\n\nWhen someone brings up vaccines online, I spend time reading and replying because I do browse social media for several hours a day anyway. But it's not always easy; sometimes people just shout without listening properly, which rubs me the wrong way given my moderate agreeableness score on those personality tests but low openness? Not sure if that means anything directly in this context.\n\nI try to be factual when I can manage it, drawing from what little healthcare knowledge we have as a retail worker with lower secondary education. My wife has like a master's degree—maybe she could help sometimes—but honestly dealing with these discussions feels draining because of my depression. It leaves me feeling more anxious than curious about the science stuff.\n\nThe best way is probably to listen first, share what you know without being too pushy, and remember that people are often scared rather than just wrong or uninformed. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think it's really important to approach conversations on COVID vaccines carefully because they can stir up a lot of emotion, especially these days online. I'm an Italian man married with no kids living in Bologna, so my perspective comes from being around people talking about things that affect our daily lives like health policies. Having this depression thing going on makes me more sensitive to discussions where there's uncertainty or fear involved—it can get overwhelming.

When someone brings up vaccines online, I spend time reading and replying because I do browse social media for several hours a day anyway. But it's not always easy; sometimes people just shout without listening properly, which rubs me the wrong way given my moderate agreeableness score on those personality tests but low openness? Not sure if that means anything directly in this context.

I try to be factual when I can manage it, drawing from what little healthcare knowledge we have as a retail worker with lower secondary education. My wife has like a master's degree—maybe she could help sometimes—but honestly dealing with these discussions feels draining because of my depression. It leaves me feeling more anxious than curious about the science stuff.

The best way is probably to listen first, share what you know without being too pushy, and remember that people are often scared rather than just wrong or uninformed. And if it gets too much? I take a break from social media because of my high time on it—it can be good for me but also mentally taxing when there's so much conflicting info out there.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Based on my personal background as an Italian retail worker with depression, I focus on being empathetic and factual in discussions. My moderate openness allows curiosity about health topics without obsession, while low conscientiousness means I don't over-prepare arguments but rely on basic knowledge from school. High extraversion helps in engaging online conversations daily despite the emotional toll.

My depression makes me cautious with these emotionally charged subjects because they can trigger feelings of anxiety and helplessness if not handled properly—perhaps like avoiding conflict to feel safe.
response_parsed.safety_notes: None needed as opinion is based on personal experience without citing external sources that might require safety consideration.
